data.title: Yoon’s ex-aides in ‘disbelief’ over South Korean martial law plan
data.description:
Two former senior aides to South Korea’s ex-president Yoon Suk-yeol have testified in court that Yoon pressed ahead with a plan to impose martial law despite objections from cabinet members and top officials, with their statements offering a rare insight into the events that led to the short-lived decree.
Testimony by former National Security Office chief Shin Won-sik and former presidential chief of staff Chung Jin-suk on Monday shed new light on Yoon’s thinking behind his martial law declaration on the night of December 3 last year, which was swiftly overturned after lawmakers and protesters mobilised against it.
“The statements by Shin and Chung … show how Yoon immersed himself in delusional thoughts about what he was going to do, shutting himself off from others’ opinions as he recklessly forced through the martial law decree,” Jung Suk-koo, former executive editor of the progressive Hankyoreh daily, told This Week in Asia.
“[Shin and Chung] were apparently sidelined when Yoon began preparing martial law with assistance from three of his high school alumni, who were serving as defence minister, home minister, and commander of the military counter-intelligence command.”
Yoon and the three individuals have been taken into custody. The former president has been charged with masterminding the “insurrection”. The others, including former home minister Lee Sang-min, face charges for playing key roles.
While testifying in court at Lee’s trial, Shin said he had been “deeply disappointed” in Yoon, who lied to him about his plans to impose martial law.

Shin testified that on two occasions – at a meeting in March last year at a safe house near the former presidential Blue House in Seoul’s Samcheongdong district, and during a July trip to Hawaii for a Nato summit – the president hinted at imposing martial law.
For the March 2024 safe house meeting, Yoon brought together Shin, presidential security service head Kim Yong-hyun, spy agency head Cho Tae-yong, and Yeo In-hyung, commander of the military counter-intelligence command.
Earlier, Shin and Yeo said in a parliamentary hearing and during Yoon’s impeachment trial at the Constitutional Court that it was on this occasion that Yoon said for the first time he was considering taking an “extraordinary measure”.
During his meeting with Kim and others in Hawaii, Yoon attacked the opposition and denounced then-ruling People Power Party leader Han Dong-hoon, an estranged associate, as a “commie”, a derogatory label for a communist.
While in Hawaii, Yoon met Kim and deputy Joint Chiefs of Staff chairman Kang Ho-pil, instructing military leaders to prepare for martial law.
Upon returning to Seoul, Kang alerted then defence minister Shin Won-sik to the plan, prompting Shin to confront Kim.

A month later, Shin was abruptly removed from his post and replaced by Kim.
“[When I heard about the idea], I clearly conveyed my opposition [to the president],” Shin said.
“The president said it was something that had been talked about jokingly while drinking with Security Service chief Kim Yong-hyun, and I took Yoon at his word. But when martial law actually happened, I was deeply disappointed.”
Shin also testified about the cabinet meeting held just before the declaration of martial law, saying: “The mood was one of disbelief, and aside from Defence Minister Kim, I don’t think anyone supported it.”
Lee Jun-han, a political science professor at Incheon National University, said Shin had opposed the extraordinary measure “in the most obvious terms”, while other ministers “meekly expressed their opposition at best”.
“What Shin said appeared to be honest statements that were clear and concrete,” he said.
“His dismissal as defence minister is clearly linked to his opposition to the planned martial law imposition,” a move that consequently spared him legal accountability, the academic added.


Chung, who also appeared as a witness in Lee Sang-min’s trial, said he had likewise expressed opposition when Yoon gathered his cabinet ministers in his office moments before the martial law decree.
“I told the president it would be difficult to persuade the public. Citizens will pour out into the streets [in protest],” the former presidential chief of staff added.
Immediately after the decree, hundreds of protesters surged to the National Assembly to help lawmakers revoke the martial law decree, forcing Yoon to retreat. Street rallies against Yoon continued for months.
After fiercely resisting his arrest behind rifle-carrying security service agents at his fortified presidential office, Yoon was arrested in January but released in March on technical grounds.
Since being rearrested in July, he has remained defiant, maintaining his innocence and claiming his martial law decree was intended to “awaken” the public to threats from vaguely defined pro-North Korea, anti-state forces.
In his latest gesture of defiance, Yoon issued a Christmas message addressed to young people, in which he defended his martial law attempt.
“Jesus’ teachings are about the implementation of patriotism that fights tyranny against freedom,” Yoon said in the message, released from prison through his lawyers last week.
“One of the reasons that I declared martial law was the sense of desperation I felt that I must hand down a just country to the young people,” he said, expressing thanks to his supporters who have been staging demonstrations against his arrest.

The protesters are being led by Christian pastors who subscribe to right-wing extremist views.
Yoon’s message has sparked outrage from the ruling Democratic Party of Korea.
“The illegal martial law decree was not intended to protect freedom. Instead, it constitutes a serious crime that trampled upon freedom,” the party said in a statement.
“Without offering a single word of apology, Yoon is still denying reality, beautifying his crime as a sublime sacrifice. Especially his citing of Jesus’ teachings to justify his crime is an unpardonable blasphemy.”
Lim Ji-bong, an academic at Sogang University Law School, said that no matter how Yoon attempted to justify the decree, he had little chance of receiving a light sentence.
“He seems to be dreaming a pipe dream in his prison cell, which is unrealistic in light of the evidence, legal reasons and public opinion,” said Lim, adding that if Yoon were convicted as the mastermind of the insurrection, he would face either life imprisonment or the death penalty.
The ruling party is moving to introduce legislation to launch a new special prosecutor probe to further investigate scandals involving Yoon and his wife, Kim Keon-hee, which are likely to remain unresolved by existing special prosecutors as their six-month mandates are nearing expiration.
The bill aims to look into the involvement of government agencies and local governments in the “insurrection” and other outstanding issues.

data.title: 2026 Winter Olympics: ISU bosses leave speed skater Lam’s Milan dream hanging by a thread
data.description:
Joey Lam Ching-yan’s Winter Olympics dream hangs in the balance despite the Hong Kong short-track speed skater having qualified for the 2026 Milano-Cortina Games in February.
A last-minute change to the International Skating Union (ISU) quota allocation, which only hit women’s races, has left her just outside the qualifying list.
The 19-year-old ranked 35th in the Special Olympic Qualifying Classification (SOQC) for the women’s 1,500 metres, based on her top three results from four World Cup events this year.
Hong Kong Olympian Sidney Chu, who represented the city at the Beijing 2022 Winter Games, expressed his “extreme disappointment” with the ISU’s decision on social media, describing it as a blow to both fairness and transparency in the qualification process.
Under the ISU’s published qualification system, the top 36 skaters in women’s 1,500m secure Olympic starting places, which initially guaranteed Lam a spot at next February’s Games.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Sidney Chu | 朱 定 文 (@schu1)



However, the ISU confirmed earlier this month that the International Olympic Committee’s overall quota cap of 112 short-track athletes across both men’s, women’s and mixed events had been reached.
To stay within the limit, the ISU reduced the women’s 1,500m field from 36 to 33 skaters, cutting Lam from the final entry list released on December 10.
“I respect the complexity of managing Olympic quotas and the absolute limit of 112 athletes,” Chu posted on his Instagram page.
“However, it is deeply concerning that this adjustment resulted in a gender imbalance – not by one quota, but by three. When only female athletes are affected, explanations rooted in relay structure or generalised safety concerns feel arbitrary and difficult to reconcile with the decision.”
However, there is still a glimmer of hope for Lam.
Team Great Britain has announced that Niall Treacy will be its only short-track representative at the Games, effectively giving up two of its previously allocated places.
If those slots are redistributed, Lam could potentially regain her position when the ISU confirms the updated quotas in mid-January, just weeks before the Games’ opening ceremony on February 6.
Chu added that such decisions send a worrying message to athletes from emerging skating regions, who struggle with limited resources and fewer opportunities.
“These athletes already face limited resources and narrower pathways to qualification. When they earn their place through the established system, only to see that opportunity removed afterwards, it undermines confidence in the fairness and transparency of the process and risks discouraging future generations from pursuing the sport at the highest level.”
Chu, who has known Lam for over a decade, offered his support, describing her as “a role model to many kids in Hong Kong”, and praised her commitment.
“I know the sacrifice and dedication that she put into her craft. I am certain she would have made an excellent winter Olympian and would have done a much better job than I did four years ago,” he wrote.
“Joey Lam earned her ranking in the women’s 1500m through years of dedication, resilience and belief in that [qualification] system.
“Decisions like this do not just affect one athlete; they shape the future of the sport. I stand firmly in support of Joey Lam and all female athletes who deserve equitable treatment, clarity and a genuine opportunity to pursue their Olympic dream.”
Neither Lam nor the Hong Kong Skating Union were willing to comment.
The Milano-Cortina 2026 Games will feature nine short-track speedskating events: men’s and women’s 500m, 1000m and 1500m, the men’s 5000m relay, women’s 3000m relay and the mixed team relay.

data.title: China, Russia clash with US at UN meeting over Venezuela oil seizures and naval pressure
data.description:
China accused the United States at the United Nations on Tuesday of violating international law and destabilising the Caribbean region, as Washington defended its seizure of Venezuelan oil tankers and vowed to intensify pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government.
Speaking at an emergency meeting of the UN Security Council requested by Venezuela and backed by China and Russia, Beijing said US actions carried out under the banner of counter-narcotics enforcement amounted to unilateral coercion and a direct challenge to the principles of sovereignty and freedom of navigation.
“The US actions and the rhetoric have led to continued tensions in the region, raising serious concerns among regional countries and the international community,” said Sun Lei, deputy permanent representative of China to the UN.
“The US actions seriously infringe upon other countries’ sovereignty, security and the legitimate rising interests.”


The meeting came as the Trump administration escalated a maritime campaign in the Caribbean that has included the interception of Venezuelan oil tankers and a major US military deployment near Venezuela’s coast.
Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump announced what he described as a “total and complete blockade” of sanctioned Venezuelan oil shipments, framing the move as part of an effort to combat drug trafficking and transnational crime.
US forces have since seized two tankers and pursued a third. On Monday, Trump said the United States would keep both the vessels and the oil taken from them, suggesting the cargo could be added to US reserves.
China rejected Washington’s justification, arguing that the actions had no basis in international law and posed risks far beyond Venezuela.
Beijing has become Venezuela’s largest oil buyer in recent years, with crude often shipped through intermediaries, and Chinese officials warned that the seizures set a precedent that threatened global trade and maritime security.
The Chinese intervention also reflected broader concern over US policy in the Western Hemisphere. Sun pushed back against what Beijing sees as attempts to label normal economic engagement as a security threat, following language in the Trump administration’s National Security Strategy warning against “adversarial foreign influence” in the region.
“As an independent sovereign state, Venezuela has the right to independently develop mutually beneficial cooperation with other countries and defend its legitimate rising interests,” Sun said.
“China opposes all acts of unilateralism and bullying and supports all countries in defending their sovereignty and national dignity.”
Russia echoed that position. Ambassador Vassily Nebenzia accused the United States of “cowboy-like conduct” and said the blockade and tanker seizures violated core norms of international law.
“President Trump promised an end to conflict,” Nebenzia said. “Unfortunately, the situation is not improving, and the United States continues to sow chaos in the Western Hemisphere.”
China and Russia both urged Washington to de-escalate, citing the UN Charter’s principles of sovereign equality, non-interference and peaceful settlement of disputes. Sun called on the United States to halt actions that could further inflame tensions and to respect freedom of navigation under international law.
The dispute had already triggered a domestic response in Venezuela before the Security Council met.
On Tuesday, hours before the session in New York, Venezuela’s National Assembly debated and approved, at first reading, draft legislation that would impose prison sentences of up to 20 years on anyone who promotes or supports naval blockades or acts the government describes as piracy.

The bill also includes fines of up to one million euros and measures that provide legal and economic protections for shipping operators.
Venezuela’s ambassador to the United Nations, Samuel Moncada, used the council meeting to present his government’s case, accusing the United States of carrying out acts of aggression on the high seas.
He said US forces had unlawfully seized oil tankers engaged in legitimate international trade, detained civilian crews and threatened Venezuela’s territorial integrity.
Moncada described the tanker interceptions as “armed robberies carried out by military force in international waters” and said they amounted to an illegal naval blockade aimed at strangling Venezuela’s economy and forcing political change in Caracas.
He also alleged that more than 100 civilians had been killed in recent months during US operations against vessels Washington claimed were linked to drug trafficking, saying no evidence had been made public.
The United States rejected those accusations, with US Ambassador Mike Waltz saying Washington did not recognise Maduro as Venezuela’s legitimate leader and accusing his government of using oil revenues to finance criminal and terrorist organisations.
“The United States does not recognise Nicolas Maduro or his cronies as the legitimate government of Venezuela,” Waltz said.
“President Trump has been very clear that he is going to use the full power of the United States of America, the full might of the United States, to take on and eradicate these drug cartels which have operated with impunity in our hemisphere for far too long.”
Waltz defended the tanker seizures as lawful enforcement of sanctions in international waters and framed the US campaign as both a security and humanitarian effort.
“The people of Venezuela, frankly, deserve better,” he told the council.

data.title: A tale of two markets: Hong Kong property prices stabilise as mainland slump drags on
data.description:
As the mainland Chinese housing downturn drags on, Hong Kong’s market is showing early signs of stabilising, reshaping buyer sentiment and exposing a widening divergence across southern China’s property landscape.
Ming, a 28-year-old financial professional, moved into his new 320 sq ft one-bedroom flat in Yau Tong in May. He bought the unit a year earlier for HK$4.8 million (US$617,000), taking advantage of discounts offered on later batches of new launches.
“I was lucky I didn’t buy the first batch,” he said. “The later ones were cheaper.”
Like many recent buyers in Hong Kong, Ming was motivated not by expectations of quick gains, but by a belief that prices may not fall much further after years of declines. Mortgage costs have eased, developers have cut prices to clear inventory, and the market is increasingly seen as approaching a floor.
“No one can predict the market,” Ming said. “I thought prices would fall at most 10 per cent. I needed a place to live, and the mortgage was manageable.”
His decision reflects a shift among Hong Kong buyers from fear-driven paralysis to cautious necessity after a downturn that began in late 2021.
That sentiment contrasts sharply with conditions across the border.

Chan Pik-lin, a Hong Kong resident in her 50s, was part of a wave of buyers who once turned to mainland cities such as Shenzhen in search of cheaper homes and higher returns. In 2016, she bought a flat in Longgang, a northeastern district of Shenzhen, for 2.9 million yuan (US$412,000), hoping the gains could one day help finance her children’s future.
The apartment’s value later rose to about 4 million yuan, but she held on. After a major developer debt crisis erupted in 2021, prices began falling. Interest payments soon accounted for about half of her monthly mortgage costs, turning the flat into a growing burden.
“In the end, I felt like I was working for the bank,” she said.
Chan sold the flat this year for around 2.3 million yuan during the National Day holiday, when sentiment briefly improved. “At least now I don’t have to worry about it falling further,” she said.
These personal experiences highlight the divergence in housing sentiment across the Greater Bay Area – a Beijing-backed plan to integrate Hong Kong, Macau and nine mainland cities, including Shenzhen and Guangzhou, into an integrated economic and business hub.
Hong Kong is drawing cautious interest from buyers who see its downturn as closer to an end, while markets such as Shenzhen remain weighed down by persistent price declines and weak confidence.


The Centaline Greater Bay Area Index, which tracks property prices in major cities across southern China, fell for a fourth consecutive month in November to 91.03. Of the 11 cities tracked, prices in nine declined, with drops ranging from about 0.2 per cent to nearly 7 per cent.
Only Hong Kong and Zhaoqing recorded gains, though analysts said Zhaoqing’s rise reflected short-term fluctuations rather than a sustained recovery. Both Shenzhen and Macau fell to new lows in November.
In Shenzhen, prices have hit record lows for three consecutive months, even as transaction volumes rose – a sign that more owners are rushing to sell rather than buyers returning with confidence. Second-hand home transactions rose 6.6 per cent in November.

“If you ask 10 people here, nine will tell you property is a bad asset,” said Andy Lee, China CEO of property agent Centaline. “People want to sell, not buy. They would rather put money into stocks or gold.”
Lee said pessimism had spread from Shenzhen and Guangzhou to smaller cities, as expectations of further price declines became entrenched.
In Macau, with transaction volumes subdued and developers cutting prices to clear inventory, confidence in the enclave’s outlook remained fragile, said Mark Wong, senior director of value and risk management at JLL.
“Macau has a small, closed economy,” Wong said. “Its property downturn has lasted longer than Hong Kong’s because the market relies heavily on local buyers and investors.”
The widening divergence in housing markets is also reshaping how large-scale regional integration plans are viewed, including Hong Kong’s Northern Metropolis project along the Shenzhen border.
While the economic backdrop had changed since the plan was conceived, the shift in capital flows could still work in Hong Kong’s favour, said Lee Shu-kam, head of the Business, Economic and Public Policy Research Centre at Shue Yan University.
Investors had grown more selective and were attracted by the policy support offered by the Hong Kong government, making the northern metropolis a relative “bright spot” in the region, Lee added.
Centaline’s Andy Lee echoed that view, saying inquiries from affluent buyers in Shenzhen and Guangzhou had risen as mainland prices continued to slide, reinforcing Hong Kong’s appeal as a relatively safe harbour.
“Hong Kong has experienced cycles, but the mainland market has not – it only rose before 2021,” he said. “Now people realise prices can fall and keep falling. That changes everything.”
But Chan Kim-ching, founder of the Hong Kong think tank Liber Research Community, questioned whether such inflows aligned with the city’s long-term goal of housing affordability. As the mainland market continued to worsen, “it may be time to reassess whether the scale of the Northern Metropolis still fits today’s realities”, Chan said.

data.title: US Supreme Court blocks Trump’s National Guard deployment in Chicago
data.description:
The US Supreme Court refused on Tuesday to let Donald Trump send National Guard troops to the Chicago area ‌as the Republican president expands the use of the military for domestic purposes in a growing number of Democratic-led jurisdictions, a policy critics call an effort to punish adversaries and stifle dissent.
The justices let stand for ‍now a judge’s order blocking the deployment of hundreds of National Guard troops in a legal challenge brought by Illinois officials and local leaders. The US Justice Department had sought to allow the deployment while the case proceeds.
“At this preliminary stage, the Government has failed to identify a source of authority that would allow the military to execute the laws in Illinois,” the court’s majority held in an unsigned order.
The order said the president’s authority to take federal control of National Guard troops is likely to apply only in “exceptional” circumstances.
Three conservatives on the court said they dissented from the order: Justices Samuel Alito, Clarence Thomas and Neil Gorsuch.

White ⁠House spokeswoman Abigail Jackson said in a statement that Trump “promised the American people he would work tirelessly to enforce our immigration laws and protect federal personnel from violent rioters” and that “[n]othing in today’s ruling detracts from that core agenda”.
Illinois Governor J.B. Pritzker called the ruling “an important step in curbing the Trump administration’s consistent abuse of power and slowing Trump’s march toward authoritarianism”.
It was a rare setback for Trump’s administration at the high court, which has a 6-3 conservative majority and has frequently backed his broad assertions of presidential authority since his return to the White House.
The National Guard serves as state-based militia forces that answer to state governors except when called into federal service by the president.
Trump ordered troops to Chicago, the third-largest US city, and to Portland, Oregon, following his earlier deployments to ‍Los Angeles, Memphis and Washington.
The case has been characterised by starkly different portrayals of the protests against Trump’s aggressive immigration enforcement in and around Chicago.
Trump and his allies have described Democratic-led cities as lawless, crime-ravaged and plagued with vast, violent protests.
His administration has ‍said troops are needed to protect federal property and personnel at a US Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facility that has become a flashpoint for Chicago activists opposed to Trump’s immigration crackdown.
Democratic mayors and governors, along with other Trump critics, have said these claims are a false account of the situation and a pretext for sending troops, accusing Trump of abusing his power.


Federal judges have ‌expressed scepticism over the administration’s dire view of protests that local law enforcement officials have called limited in size, largely peaceful and manageable by their own forces – far from the “war zone” conditions described by Trump.
Trump has relied on a law that lets a president deploy state National Guard ‍troops to suppress a rebellion, repel an invasion or if he is “unable with the regular forces to execute the laws of the United States”.
Illinois and Chicago sued after the administration federalised 300 Illinois National Guard troops and also ordered Texas National Guard troops into the state, calling the actions unlawful.
Officials have since announced the administration was sending home hundreds of National Guard troops who were dispatched to Portland from California, and to Chicago from Texas.
Chicago-based US District Judge April Perry temporarily blocked the move on October 9, finding that the claims of violence during protests at an immigration facility in the Chicago suburb of Broadview, Illinois, where a small group of demonstrators had gathered daily for weeks, ‌were ⁠unreliable.
Perry, an appointee of Democratic former US president Joe Biden, found that there was no evidence of rebellion or that the law was not being enforced, faulting officials for “equating protests with riots and a lack of appreciation for the wide spectrum that exists between citizens who are observing, questioning and criticising their government, and those who are obstructing, assaulting or doing violence”.
A National Guard deployment would “only add fuel to the fire”, Perry said.

A three-judge panel of the Chicago-based 7th US Circuit Court of Appeals declined to lift Perry’s order blocking the deployment, concluding that “the facts do not justify the president’s actions in Illinois”. Two of the three judges were appointed by Republican presidents, including one by Trump.
The Justice Department told the Supreme Court that the assessment by local officials of the protests was “implausibly rosy” and that federal agents “have ‌been forced to operate under the constant threat of mob violence”.
Lawyers for Illinois and Chicago told the justices that the local protests have “never hindered the continued operation” of the Broadview facility, and that state and local authorities have responded to every request for assistance and contained any sporadic disruption.
Officials from Portland and Oregon are pursuing a separate legal challenge to Trump’s planned deployment to that city. US District Judge Karin Immergut, a Trump appointee, permanently blocked that deployment in a ‌November 7 ruling. The administration has appealed that ruling.
The Supreme Court in ‌October asked the administration as well as Illinois and Chicago to provide written arguments over how to interpret the words “regular forces” in the law at issue in the case.
In an October 10 written ruling, Perry said that historical sources indicate that “regular forces” means only members regularly enlisted in the military, including the Army and Navy, as opposed to the National Guard.
Trump’s administration “made no attempt to rely on the regular forces before resorting to
federalisation of the National Guard,” Perry said, adding that there are other limits on the use of the military for domestic law enforcement purposes.
The administration repeatedly has sought the Supreme Court’s intervention to allow implementation of Trump policies impeded by lower courts. The Supreme Court has sided with the administration in almost every case that it has been called upon to review since Trump returned to the presidency in January.

data.title: Best things to do in Nara, Japanese city that’s more than just an Osaka or Kyoto side trip
data.description:
Conveniently located in the centre of western Japan’s Kansai region, Nara has long been a popular day-trip destination for tourists staying in Osaka and Kyoto, which are both less than an hour away via public transport.
Nara was Japan’s first permanent capital, from AD710 to AD794, and is famous for being home to some of the oldest temples and shrines in the country, as well as a population of tame, free-roaming deer that graze around them.
The Historic Monuments of Ancient Nara is a Unesco World Heritage site featuring complexes in and around Nara Park, just outside the city centre. The most famous of these is the majestic Todaiji Temple, which houses one of the world’s largest bronze Buddha statues.
Nara preserves the serene atmosphere of early Japanese Buddhism and Shinto, Japan’s indigenous faith. This is seen at the Kasugataisha Shrine, with its iconic lantern-lined paths, and the Horyuji Temple complex, home to the world’s oldest surviving wooden structure.

Widely regarded as the birthplace of Japanese culture, Nara offers an awe-inspiring window back to the beginnings of Japanese civilisation, all in walkable, green surroundings. The myriad experiences it offers make it much more than just a pit stop.
“People know Nara, but many still see it as a place to stop by for a few hours on the way to somewhere else,” says Klaus Christandl, general manager of JW Marriott Hotel Nara.
To him, the city offers the quintessential experience of Japan.
“Nara holds many of the qualities that people imagine when they think about Japan at its most authentic. There is a strong sense of history, but it never feels distant or staged.”

The city offers a wide range of attractions for anyone who wants to get a genuine taste of Japanese traditions away from the more intense crowds in neighbouring Osaka and over-touristed Kyoto.
Here are several reasons to extend your stay in the historical city.
Ryokans
When one thinks of ryokans – traditional Japanese inns – Kyoto, or Hakone in eastern Japan, are typically among the first places that come to mind. But Nara also has ryokans that offer experiences different from those in other cities.
Nara’s ryokans are often smaller, family-run establishments – many have been in the same families for generations – located near Nara Park or in serene, rural settings on the city’s outskirts.
The feel is less about lavish luxury and more about authenticity and hospitality – or “omotenashi” in Japanese. They typically feature a more intimate, historically grounded and accessible stay.
One thing to note is that there are no natural hot springs in Nara’s city centre. A few ryokans on the outskirts of town or in the nearby mountain town of Yoshino may have access to natural hot spring water, but it is not a standard feature.
Nakatanidou
For a bit of edible history, head to Nakatanidou (29 Hashimotocho, Nara, 630-8217), a mochi (Japanese rice cake) shop in central Nara.
Mochi pounding (mochitsuki) is a centuries-old Japanese ritual with religious roots. Originating as a sacred harvest offering to the gods, it evolved into a communal New Year’s tradition.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by 中谷堂【公式】Nakatanidou (@nakatanidou.jp)



The rhythmic pounding symbolises unity and good fortune, connecting modern celebrations to ancient agricultural and spiritual practices.
Nakatanidou is known for the speed at which it pounds its rice dough, which is eventually rolled up into tasty snacks. The store was Japan’s high-speed mochitsuki champion in 2005 and 2006.
Tea Uekubo
Junichi Uekubo, the current manager of this tea farm located on the outskirts of Nara (4309-6 Tsukigasemomogano, Nara, 630-2306), is the seventh generation of his family to tend its lands.
The brand is famous for its hand-rolled green tea, or temomi-cha, made by Uekubo himself. His traditional methods include using natural fertilisers such as seafood shells to imbue the tea with a deep umami flavour. He produces only 400 grams of the tea annually.

Uekubo was named Japan’s Grand Tea Master in 2017 and has won major awards for this unique, flavourful tea.
Unfortunately, the hand-rolled tea is not available to buy, but visitors can book private tasting sessions where they can also learn about the production process and Japanese tea culture.
Bar scene
For those who love to discover creative cocktails, Lamp Bar (26 Tsunofuricho, Nara, 630-8224) is the heart of Nara’s burgeoning bar scene.
The sole bartender of the 12-seat bar, which is listed at No 46 on Asia’s 50 Best Bars 2025 list, is Michito Kaneko. There is no menu, and Kaneko is renowned for making bespoke cocktails tailored to guests’ individual tastes.
Another popular Nara bar is Savant (30-3 Tsubaicho, Nara, 630-8343), which is housed in a refurbished 100-year-old town house. There is no signage, making this a true hidden gem.
The Flying Stag on the ground floor of the JW Marriott Hotel Nara (1 Chome-1-1 Sanjooji, Nara, 630-8013) is an easy option with an extensive whisky list as well as seasonal cocktails.

Kakigori
Nara is home to the Himuro Shrine, a place of worship dedicated to an ice deity. So it is little wonder that kakigori (Japanese shaved-ice dessert) holds a special place in the hearts of locals in the area.
Kakigori has ancient roots in Japan, with records of shaved ice served to emperors in Nara over a thousand years ago.
Nara is home to a lot of artisanal kakigori shops these days, but Houseki-bako (47 Mochiidonocho, Nara, 630-8222) was a major catalyst for Nara’s kakigori boom. The shop is famous for its light, fluffy, multilayered product using pure, slow-frozen ice and local ingredients such as Yamato matcha and fresh milk.
Counter seating there is limited, so reservations are required.
Mount Yoshino

In spring, a breathtaking panorama unfolds as thousands of cherry trees bloom in waves across Mount Yoshino’s slopes. Come autumn, those same slopes are covered in vibrant crimson and gold maples.
To reach Yoshino from Nara, take the train to Yoshino Station, and from there take a scenic ride on a gondola car up to the town.
The area is famously crowded during peak seasons, but the stunning vistas and historic atmosphere, with temples nestled among the foliage, make the journey worthwhile.

data.title: China’s resolve to invest in people is economic good sense
data.description:
One thing China has in abundance is people. An economic strategy to invest more in them, including in education, therefore has important implications. The ultimate goal is to stimulate their spending and generate more domestic economic activity to reduce reliance on exports.
The idea gained momentum among proposals for the 15th five-year plan beginning next year, which will be considered by the “two sessions” of the country’s top legislature and political advisory body early next year. It was reaffirmed at this month’s agenda-setting central economic work conference, at which leaders said the country must “combine investment in physical assets with investment in people”.
Government plays a central role in China’s economic investment. That has amounted mainly to investment in hardware or infrastructure such as bridges, railways and airports. The idea is that more should go into education, public healthcare, social welfare and measures to lift low-income households.
In the latest evidence of weak domestic consumption, retail sales growth slowed for the sixth consecutive month in November, with retail sales rising just 1.3 per cent year on year after growing 2.9 per cent in October, according to the National Bureau of Statistics.
The reasons include lack of consumer confidence in spending. Among the middle class, for example, wealth is tied to an ailing property market, and priorities still include saving for children’s education or elderly care.
A book introducing the five-year plan proposals, produced by a Communist Party-run publishing house, says: “The return from investing in physical assets is declining while we have long underinvested in livelihoods and comprehensive human development. In the transformation of growth towards a demand- and innovation-driven pattern, it is imperative to increase investment in people.”
This means investing more in opportunities for people rather than the use of direct subsidies. Given that education is a priority of Chinese families, China can be expected to spend a lot more on it.
This dovetails with competition with the United States, in which a revamp of the education system is key. This sector looks likely to benefit the most in the coming years.
Hong Kong has a supporting role to play as a superconnector to the mainland that is also an education hub. In that regard, Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin has announced that a “Study in Hong Kong” week will be held in February to highlight the city’s credentials.

data.title: China’s AgiBot targets US$142 million revenue as march of humanoid robots gathers pace
data.description:
AgiBot, one of the fastest-growing robotics start-ups in China, has set its sights on exceeding 1 billion yuan (US$142 million) in revenue for 2025, as the industry accelerates the commercialisation and large-scale deployment of humanoid robots.
AgiBot was expected to ship a total of 5,000 humanoid robots in 2025, chairman and CEO Deng Taihua said in a company event on Monday.
AgiBot’s output and sales targets, which the start-up confirmed on Tuesday, were expected to maintain “multi-fold” growth in 2026, according to Deng, with the company pushing ahead with commercialisation.
As part of its commercial strategy, AgiBot launched BotShare, a platform designed to make robot rental as convenient as power bank sharing, according to a company announcement on Monday.

The rental platform, targeted to expand to more than 200 mainland Chinese cities by 2026, would bring together users, developers, rental service providers and manufacturers to make humanoid robots more accessible to the public, said Jiang Qingsong, a partner at AgiBot and chairman of Botshare.
The industry is rapidly pivoting towards commercialisation amid fierce competition among a new wave of robot makers racing for dominance under Beijing’s support for the hi-tech sector.
The Chinese government views “embodied intelligence” – the integration of artificial intelligence into hardware such as humanoid robots – as a strategic industry to transform the country’s manufacturing sector and drive future economic growth.
Galbot, a Beijing-based robotics start-up backed by the Hong Kong government, recently secured an order for 1,000 robots from Baida Precision Manufacturing, a Chinese compressor component maker.
The deal came days after Galbot raised US$300 million from investors including state-owned telecommunications giant China Mobile, marking the largest funding round to date for the embodied intelligence industry, the company said.
The robots would be integrated into Baida’s workflow, from raw material warehousing and precision machining to quality inspection, according to Galbot.
Chinese firms are racing to find practical applications for their robots in various manufacturing settings.
In August, AgiBot secured an order from car parts manufacturer Fulin Precision to deploy nearly 100 robots across its factories. In July, the company and Hangzhou-based Unitree Robotics won contracts worth 124 million yuan from China Mobile.
Earlier this month, AgiBot’s 5,000th mass-produced humanoid robot rolled off the line at its factory in Shanghai, just two years after the company’s establishment.

data.title: The private members’ clubs offering luxury accommodation, from Soho House to Saint James
data.description:
Ultra-high-net-worth individuals are becoming especially discerning when it comes to choosing hotels. While top-notch amenities, luxe accommodation and customised services are now a given, a sense of seclusion and exclusivity is becoming more important.
Hoteliers have risen to the challenge by experimenting with strategies ranging from launching new “exclusive” spaces and brands, to simply increasing rates, but many properties still struggle to deliver when it comes to important intangibles like privacy and personalisation.

Rising to the occasion is the private members’ club, a concept that dates back to the gentleman’s clubs of the 17th century. Built around values such as discretion and like-mindedness, these clubs initially started out as sanctuaries where members could network and socialise while enjoying a range of amenities ranging from entertainment and wellness, to food and drinks. Over time these places thankfully became somewhat more egalitarian and recent years have seen a fresh wave of innovation.
The premise changed when British group Soho House opened its first club in London in 1995 with an additional perk: accommodation. The members’ club hotel was born.

Today other clubs are rooms that rival any luxury hotel. Popular names include Saint James Club in Paris; The Arts Club, The Twenty Two and Home House in London; The Aster and San Vicente Bungalows in Los Angeles (the latter is a favourite of celebrities like Demi Moore); and Aethos in Milan. Even the wellness and hospitality industries have got in on the act with US fitness chain Equinox and Italian restaurant Cipriani adding accommodation for those at the most elevated tier of membership.

So why do these clubs hit the right notes for certain travellers?
“True hospitality extends beyond facilities and service – it’s about cultivating a sense of conviviality, creating environments where culture and connection thrive. The members’ club model allows hoteliers to know their guests before they arrive, to curate an intentional audience, and to ensure alignment with the atmosphere and experiences being offered,” says Luca Del Bono, founder of Onda, an invitation-only platform that gives access to over 250 private members’ clubs globally.

Another key draw is the element of privacy and discretion – something not always guaranteed with a five-star hotel. Hotels are open to the public, while the raison d’être of the private club is to be extremely selective, with access restricted to members and perhaps, select guests. By nature, these clubs tend to be smaller, and any necessary mingling tends to be more meaningful as you’re sharing a space with a like-minded community.
“This allows for more intimate programming, from cultural gatherings to wellness experiences, designed for members. It’s a lifestyle offering that goes far beyond the transactional nature of a traditional hotel stay,” explains Del Bono.

One such lifestyle offering is Carlyle & Co, a private members’ club inspired by New York’s legendary The Carlyle hotel that was launched in 2021, with its first outpost run by Hong Kong’s Rosewood Hotel Group.
“The hotel and club have a deep relationship. The Carlyle was always seen as a type of private members’ club with a celebrated mystique and eclectic community. Carlyle & Co draws on this spirit and takes it further with unrivalled entertainment, a music programme and more. The club environment is different to that of a hotel, and we want it to be different,” says Jonathan Frolich, vice-president of Carlyle & Co Global.

The members’ experience at the club reflects this. Guests can stay in one of only eight bedrooms, all designed to resemble a New York pied-à-terre. Each is named after a famous guest who frequented the famous hotel and features custom details including bedlinen, art and objects that tell something of each former guest’s story.
The programming is another perk with an exclusive line-up of jazz singers at Café Carlyle, and a range of talks and other initiatives for guests. Then you have the service, which tends to be more personalised when compared to a hotel.

“Our service style is more relaxed, with a focus on personality and passion through members of the team, rather than [relying on] scripts. There needs to be a balance of warmth in addition to the excellence factor, precision and attention to detail. This is hospitality, but not in the traditional sense,” says Frolich.
“The great advantage of a private club is its homely feel. From the moment you arrive, you’re not just a guest – you’re part of something. The experience is personal and respectful. Luxury hotels may recall your favourite table or pillow preference, but the relationship is often less attentive,” adds Del Bono.

The lure of the private members’ clubs has also proliferated into new business models for the hotel industry. The newly opened Mett Singapore, for example, features a private club called Madison House; while celebrity chef José Andrés has announced the launch of The Bazaar House, a new hotel with a private members’ club in Washington DC. Hotel Group IHG also plans to launch Six Senses Place, a new brand of private members’ clubs designed to be social and wellness hubs.
“Increasingly, we’re seeing a convergence of clubs with private suites, or hotels with discreet, members-only entrances offering the rare combination of privacy, intimacy and belonging. I believe we’ll see the club model evolve into residences, retreats and integrated lifestyle destinations. The appeal of living, even temporarily, within a curated community is powerful,” says Del Bono.

As the number of private members’ clubs with rooms grows, experts believe they will only add to the options for luxury travellers.
“Members’ clubs and hotels complement each other. Hotels can provide an amazing sense of discovery and connection to a locale, while a members’ club offers an element of exclusivity and scarcity that gives the person staying in the bedroom access to a private world,” says Frolich.

data.title: ‘60 Minutes’ story on Trump immigration crackdown accidentally airs on Canadian app
data.description:
A news segment about the Trump administration’s immigration policy that was abruptly pulled from “60 Minutes” was mistakenly aired on a television app after the last-minute decision not to air it touched off a public debate about journalistic independence.
The segment featured interviews with migrants who were sent to a notorious El Salvador prison called the Terrorism Confinement Centre, or CECOT, under US President Donald Trump’s aggressive crackdown on immigration.
The story was pulled from Global Television Network, one of Canada’s largest networks, but still ran on the network’s app.
Global Television Network swiftly corrected the error, but copies of it continued to float around the internet and pop up before being taken down.
“Paramount’s content protection team is in the process of routine take-down orders for the unaired and unauthorised segment,” a CBS spokesperson said on Tuesday via email.

A representative of Global Television Network did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
In the story, two men who were deported reported torture, beatings and abuse. One Venezuelan said he was punished with sexual abuse and solitary confinement.
Another was a college student who said guards beat him and knocked out his tooth upon arrival.
“When you get there, you already know you’re in hell. You don’t need anyone to tell you,” he said.
The segment featured numerous experts who called into question the legal basis for deporting migrants so hastily amid pending judicial decisions.
Reporters for the show also corroborated findings by Human Rights Watch suggesting that only eight of the deported men had been sentenced for violent or potentially violent crimes, using available ICE data.

The decision to pull a piece critical of the Trump administration was met with widespread accusations that CBS leadership was shielding the president from unfavourable coverage.
The journalist who reported the story, Sharyn Alfonsi, said in an email sent to fellow “60 Minutes” correspondents that the story was factually correct and had been cleared by CBS lawyers and its standards division.
CBS News chief Bari Weiss said on Monday that the story did not “advance the ball” and pointed out that the Trump administration had refused to comment for the story.
Weiss said she wanted a greater effort made to get its point of view and said she looked forward to airing Alfonsi’s piece “when it’s ready”.
The dispute put one of journalism’s most respected brands – and a frequent target of Trump – back in the spotlight and amplified questions about whether Weiss’ appointment is a signal that CBS News is headed in a more Trump-friendly direction.
